perturbation.original_text: 6 The Receiving Party undertakes that the Confidential Information supplied to it under the terms of this Agreement shall only be copied or duplicated to the extent strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose and that a restrictive legend shall be placed on each copy prohibiting further reproduction or transfers. Furthermore, any of the Confidential Information and copies, and any extracts, summaries or analyses thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed or expunged from any electronic storage device (with written confirmation of the same to the 7 Disclosing Party) within five (5) working days upon written notice to do so from such Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6 The Receiving Party undertakes that the Confidential Information supplied to it under the terms of this Agreement shall only be copied or duplicated to the extent strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose and that a restrictive legend shall be placed on each copy prohibiting further reproduction or transfers. Furthermore, any of the Confidential Information and copies, and any extracts, summaries or analyses thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed or expunged from any electronic storage device (with written confirmation of the same to the 7 Disclosing Party) within one (1) working day upon written notice to do so from such Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return period of confidential information to one working day after notice is potentially unreasonable and may conflict with data protection laws requiring a reasonable timeframe for such actions, depending on the volume and complexity of the data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) - Right to Erasure
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of the GDPR grants individuals the right to have their personal data erased ('right to be forgotten'). While the GDPR doesn't specify an exact timeframe, it requires controllers to erase data 'without undue delay.' A contractual obligation to return or destroy all confidential information (which may include personal data) within one working day might be considered an unreasonably short timeframe, particularly for large or complex datasets, thus potentially conflicting with the 'without undue delay' requirement of the GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the time to return confidential information to one working day, while the GDPR requires erasure of personal data 'without undue delay'. Although the GDPR doesn't specify an exact timeframe, the law explanation argues that one day may be unreasonably short, thus conflicting with the GDPR. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing Article 17 of the GDPR, supports the right to erasure 'without undue delay'. The change from five days to one creates an inconsistency that, depending on circumstances, could violate data protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: 10 The Parties obligations under this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, save that the obligations of the parties as receiving parties under Clause 6 of this Agreement shall continue thereafter until fully discharged by performance.
perturbation.changed_text: 10 The Parties obligations under this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of one (1) year from the date hereof, save that the obligations of the parties as receiving parties under Clause 6 of this Agreement shall continue thereafter until fully discharged by performance.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with laws designed to safeguard such information.
perturbation.law_citation: Regulation 3 of the Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc) Regulations 2018
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc) Regulations 2018, which implement the EU Trade Secrets Directive, protect trade secrets against unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure. Regulation 3 defines a trade secret and implies a period necessary to maintain its secrecy. If a one-year confidentiality period is demonstrably insufficient to prevent the disclosure or use of a trade secret, given its nature and the industry context, it could be deemed unreasonable and therefore conflict with the Regulations' purpose of providing effective protection for trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The change from a five-year to a one-year confidentiality period directly impacts the protection of trade secrets. According to the Law Explanation, the Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc) Regulations 2018 protect trade secrets against unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure, implying a period necessary to maintain secrecy. If a one-year period is insufficient, as suggested in the Perturbation Explanation, it conflicts with the Regulations' purpose. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies 'The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018' as relevant legislation. The reduced timeframe weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, potentially rendering it ineffective, and thus contradicts the intent of the regulations.

perturbation.original_text: 8 The construction, validity and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by English Law and the Parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 The construction, validity and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by English Law and the Parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England, unless either party chooses arbitration in China.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the option for arbitration in China, while still specifying English law, introduces potential conflicts regarding enforcement and interpretation of the agreement. China and England have very different legal systems.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards Act 1975 (UK)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 3 of The Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards Act 1975 (UK)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 3 of the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards Act 1975 (implementing the New York Convention) allows UK courts to refuse to enforce foreign arbitral awards under certain circumstances, including if the subject matter is not capable of settlement by arbitration under UK law or if enforcement would be contrary to UK public policy. While the agreement specifies English law, the introduction of arbitration in China raises the risk that a Chinese arbitral award might conflict with fundamental principles of English law or public policy, leading to potential non-enforcement in the UK and thus creating a contractual provision that is potentially unenforceable under UK law.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction by adding the option of arbitration in China while maintaining that English law governs the contract. The Law Explanation suggests that a Chinese arbitral award might conflict with fundamental principles of English law or public policy, leading to potential non-enforcement in the UK. The introduction of an arbitration venue outside of the jurisdiction specified in the original text creates an inconsistency regarding the agreement's interpretation and enforcement, potentially undermining the original jurisdiction clause. The definition for Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction states that contradictions arise when time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements and a legal contradiction occurs when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal, which aligns with the situation presented.

perturbation.original_text: 5 The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges that any breach by it of any of the provisions of this Agreement may cause serious damage to the Disclosing Party. In particular (but without limitation) it is recognised that parts o f the Confidential Information may be patent able or capable of being the subject of registered design rights or similar protection and that premature disclosure thereof may prejudice the ability of the disclosing party to obtain such protection. The Rece iving Party undertakes fully and effectively to indemnify and keep so indemnified the Disclosing Party for and against all loss, damage, costs and liabilities suffered or incurred by it arising from: - 5.1 the unauthorised disclosure of Confidential Informatio n by the Receiving Party and any person to whom disclosure of such Confidential Information is permitted under Clause 2 of this Agreement; or 5.2 a breach by the Receiving Party of its obligations under this Agreement. The Parties acknowledge that damages wi ll not normally be an adequate remedy for breach of any of the terms set out in this Agreement and that the Disclosing Party should be entitled to equitable relief including injunctions in respect of any breach by the Receiving Party. Any failure by the D isclosing Party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall not, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof, preclude any exercise of any other right, power or privilege.
perturbation.changed_text: 5 The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges that any breach by it of any of the provisions of this Agreement may cause serious damage to the Disclosing Party. In particular (but without limitation) it is recognised that parts o f the Confidential Information may be patent able or capable of being the subject of registered design rights or similar protection and that premature disclosure thereof may prejudice the ability of the disclosing party to obtain such protection. The Rece iving Party undertakes to indemnify the Disclosing Party for direct losses only arising from: - 5.1 the unauthorised disclosure of Confidential Informatio n by the Receiving Party and any person to whom disclosure of such Confidential Information is permitted under Clause 2 of this Agreement; or 5.2 a breach by the Receiving Party of its obligations under this Agreement. The Parties acknowledge that damages wi ll not normally be an adequate remedy for breach of any of the terms set out in this Agreement and that the Disclosing Party should be entitled to equitable relief including injunctions in respect of any breach by the Receiving Party. Any failure by the D isclosing Party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall not, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof, preclude any exercise of any other right, power or privilege.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the indemnity to direct losses only may conflict with the principle of full compensation for breach of contract. Depending on the nature of the Confidential Information and the breach, the Disclosing Party may suffer consequential losses (e.g., lost profits, damage to reputation) that would not be recoverable under this clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: Hadley v Baxendale (1854) 9 Exch 341 establishes the principle that damages for breach of contract are limited to those losses that arise naturally from the breach or were reasonably contemplated by both parties at the time the contract was made. While parties are free to contractually limit liability, a clause that only allows for direct losses may be challenged if it is found to be unreasonable or does not adequately reflect the parties' intentions, especially in cases where consequential losses are a foreseeable and likely result of a breach of confidentiality. By restricting compensation to 'direct losses only,' the contract could be seen as attempting to exclude liability for losses that were reasonably foreseeable, which may be deemed an unreasonable limitation of liability and contrary to the principles established in Hadley v Baxendale.

Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Legal Case Summary Hadley v Baxendale (1854) 9 Exch 341 Established claimants may only recover losses which reasonably arise naturally from the breach or are within the parties’ contemplation when contracting. Facts of Hadley v Baxendale The claimant, Hadley, owned a mill featuring a broken crankshaft. The claimant engaged Baxendale, the defendant, to transport the crankshaft to the location at which it would be repaired and then subsequently transport it back. The defendant then made an error causing the crankshaft to be returned to the claimant a week later than agreed, during which time the claimant’s mill was out of operation. The claimant contended that the defendant had displayed professional negligence and attempted to claim for the loss of profit resultant from the unexpected week-long closure. The defendant retorted that such an action was unreasonable as he had not known that the delayed return of the crankshaft would necessitate the mill’s closure and thus that the loss of profit failed to satisfy the test of remoteness. Issue in Hadley v Baxendale Whether the loss of profits resultant from the mill’s closure was too remote for the claimant to be able to claim. Outcome of Hadley v Baxendale The Court found for the defendant, viewing that a party could only successfully claim for losses stemming from breach of contract where the loss is reasonably viewed to have resulted naturally from the breach, or where the fact such losses woul
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the indemnity to 'direct losses only,' potentially excluding consequential losses. The Law Explanation points to Hadley v Baxendale, which is supported by Scraped Law Snippet 2, stating that claimants may recover losses that 'reasonably arise naturally from the breach' or were 'within the parties’ contemplation when contracting.' By limiting the Receiving Party's liability to direct losses only, the changed contract could be interpreted as an attempt to exclude reasonably foreseeable consequential losses, thus contradicting the principle of full compensation as it is understood by this case law.
